doctrines.rule: An agreement to commit a crime may be inferred from coordinated conduct and surrounding circumstances even in the absence of an explicit verbal or written pact.
levers: Spikes in communication during key events
Presence at key locations via cell data
traps: Assuming a formal 'meeting of the minds' is required
Confusing mere presence with coordinated action

doctrines.rule: A defendant must act with the specific purpose of promoting or facilitating the commission of the object crime, regardless of the mental state required for the object crime itself.
doctrines.split: Some jurisdictions allow knowledge for 'aggravated' crimes, but purpose is the standard rule.
traps: Applying a 'reckless' or 'negligent' standard to the agreement
Confusing knowledge of a crime with intent to join it

doctrines.rule: A conspirator or coconspirator must perform an act in pursuance of the conspiracy, though the act itself may be entirely legal and trivial.
doctrines.split: MPC requires an overt act only for 3rd, 4th, or 5th degree felonies; 18 U.S.C. § 371 requires it for all.
levers: Legality of the act (e.g., buying a plane ticket)
Remoteness of the act from the crime
traps: Requiring a 'substantial step' as in attempt law
Thinking the defendant themselves must perform the act (any member's act suffices)

doctrines.rule: Under the unilateral approach, a conspiracy exists if the defendant believes they are agreeing with another, even if the other party is a feigning government agent.
doctrines.split: Traditional/Bilateral (requires two genuine minds) vs. MPC/Unilateral (focuses on defendant's belief).
levers: Status of the coconspirator (undercover agent vs. genuine criminal)
traps: Applying the bilateral rule in an MPC jurisdiction to dismiss a sting operation charge

doctrines.rule: A conspirator must specifically intend that every element of the target offense be accomplished, including aggravating factors like weapon use.
doctrines.split: Pond (requires intent for all elements) vs. Dissent (only requires the mens rea of the target offense).
traps: Assuming conspiracy to commit a strict liability crime doesn't require intent for the strict liability element

doctrines.rule: Conspiracy cannot be maintained for crimes defined by an unintended result, such as crimes of negligence.
levers: Nature of the target offense (negligent homicide vs. intentional murder)
traps: Charging conspiracy to commit involuntary manslaughter

doctrines.rule: A supplier of legal goods/services is not a conspirator based on mere knowledge of criminal use unless they have a 'stake in the venture' or the crime is 'aggravated.'
traps: Assuming high-volume sales to criminals automatically equals conspiracy

doctrines.rule: A conspirator is liable for substantive crimes committed by coconspirators if the crimes were foreseeable and in furtherance of the conspiracy.
doctrines.split: Federal/Pinkerton (vicarious liability) vs. MPC (rejects Pinkerton; requires accomplice liability).
levers: Whether the defendant was in custody/prison at the time
Whether the act furthered the specific goal
traps: Applying Pinkerton in an MPC jurisdiction
Failing to check if the crime was a 'natural consequence'

doctrines.rule: A single conspiracy requires a 'rim' connecting the 'spokes' of a wheel—a common purpose or mutual stake—rather than just a common central figure.
traps: Treating multiple independent frauds as one conspiracy because they use the same broker

doctrines.rule: A simple agreement to buy or sell contraband for personal use does not constitute a conspiracy without evidence of a shared purpose to further distribution.
traps: Charging a one-time buyer of drugs with conspiracy to distribute

doctrines.rule: An agreement to commit a crime that by definition requires two participants (e.g., adultery, bribery) cannot be prosecuted as conspiracy unless additional parties are involved.
doctrines.split: Judicial presumption; can be overridden by clear legislative intent.
levers: Number of participants relative to the minimum required for the crime
traps: Applying the rule to crimes that *could* be committed by one person but happened to involve two

doctrines.rule: A person belonging to a class protected or deliberately excluded by a statute cannot be prosecuted for conspiracy to violate that statute.
levers: Statutory purpose (e.g., protecting victims of trafficking)
traps: Charging a minor with conspiracy to violate a law protecting minors

cases.name: State v. Pond
cases.holding: Conspiracy requires specific intent for every element of the target offense, including aggravating factors like the display of a weapon.
cases.facts_summary: Defendant acted as a lookout for a robbery where a coconspirator gestured as if he had a gun; defendant claimed no knowledge or intent regarding the weapon.

cases.name: People v. Lauria
cases.holding: Knowledge of a customer's criminal activity is insufficient for conspiracy liability unless the supplier has a stake in the venture or the crime is aggravated.
cases.facts_summary: An answering service owner knew some of his clients were prostitutes but charged them standard rates and had no other involvement.

cases.name: Pinkerton v. United States
cases.holding: A conspirator is vicariously liable for substantive offenses committed by coconspirators in furtherance of the conspiracy and within its foreseeable scope.
cases.facts_summary: One brother committed tax evasion while the other was in prison; the imprisoned brother was held liable for the substantive crimes based on their ongoing conspiracy.

cases.name: Interstate Circuit v. United States
cases.holding: Agreement can be inferred where competitors adhere to a proposed plan knowing that concerted action is required for the plan's success.
cases.facts_summary: Movie distributors all received a letter naming each other as recipients and simultaneously changed their business practices to match the letter's demands.

cases.name: Kotteakos v. United States
cases.holding: A single conspiracy cannot be charged when multiple groups deal independently with a central figure without a common purpose or mutual connection (a 'rimless wheel').
cases.facts_summary: Thirty-two defendants were charged in one conspiracy for obtaining fraudulent loans through the same broker, despite not knowing each other.

blocks.summary: The Dan Markel murder-for-hire case study illustrating inference of agreement and vicarious liability.
Analysis of the split between bilateral and unilateral conspiracy theories in the context of undercover stings.
State v. Pond and the requirement of specific intent for all elements of the object crime.
People v. Lauria and the distinction between knowledge and purpose for commercial suppliers.
Comparison table of Silk Road vs. Social Media platforms under the Lauria factors.
Pinkerton v. United States establishing vicarious liability for coconspirators.
Bank robbery hypothetical illustrating the expansive reach of Pinkerton vs. accomplice liability.
Interstate Circuit v. United States regarding conscious parallelism and inferred agreement.
Kotteakos v. United States and the 'rimless wheel' problem in large-scale prosecutions.
Structural limits on conspiracy including Wharton's Rule and the Gebardi principle.
Discussion of corporate conspiracy and the Purdue Pharma case.
Analysis of drug stings, manufactured conspiracy, and the 'girlfriend problem' in sentencing.
System architecture critique involving asset forfeiture and proactive policing.

hypo_seeds.description: A lookout in a robbery where a partner unexpectedly uses a toy gun that looks real.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests whether the student applies the Pond rule requiring specific intent for the 'display' element.

hypo_seeds.description: A chemical supplier sells large quantities of precursor chemicals to a known drug lab at 3x market price.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the 'stake in the venture' (inflated prices) inference from Lauria.

hypo_seeds.description: A ringleader hires two separate hitmen who don't know each other to kill the same target.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the 'rim' requirement—is this one conspiracy or two?

hypo_seeds.description: A defendant agrees with an undercover cop to buy stolen goods, but the goods aren't actually stolen.
hypo_seeds.note: Tests the bilateral vs. unilateral split regarding feigning coconspirators.
